talk_title: Evolution of PARP inhibitors and lessons for synthetic lethality development

biomarkers.name: RAD51 foci
biomarkers.finding: Low RAD51 foci levels correlated with tumor regression in PARP inhibitor-treated patient-derived models

biomarkers.name: HRD genomic scar
biomarkers.finding: HRD scar associated with improved PFS on olaparib maintenance in first-line ovarian cancer

models.name: patient-derived xenograft cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: various HR alterations, RAD51 foci measured

MOA_summary: PARP inhibitors trap PARP1 on DNA, preventing repair of single-strand breaks that convert to double-strand breaks during replication; in HR-deficient tumors (e.g., BRCA1/2 loss) this leads to lethal genomic instability, while normal cells retain homologous recombination capacity, providing a therapeutic window. Trapping potency, dose intensity, and duration of target inhibition dictate clinical efficacy.
key_findings: PARP1 trapping, not just catalytic inhibition, is essential for monotherapy activity; talazoparib and sarucaparib show stronger trapping and greater efficacy than rucaparib.
Complete (>90%) PARP inhibition is required for tumor regression; sub‑therapeutic dosing leads to tumor escape and resistance.
BRCA1/2 mutation type influences response; hypomorphic C61G BRCA1 mutants confer reduced sensitivity compared to null alleles.
Acquired resistance is driven by HR re‑activation, evidenced by RAD51 foci re‑appearance in >90% of resistant models.
Dose‑intensity correlates with long‑term tumor control; continuous high‑dose olaparib prevents escape, whereas intermittent dosing increases residual tumor burden.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PARP1 L77P mutation abolishes trapping and confers resistance to olaparib and talazoparib.
Reversion mutations restoring HR function lead to RAD51 foci resurgence and PARP inhibitor resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergistic DNA damage in HRD tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression in preclinical models

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synthetic lethal interaction in BRCA‑mutant context

open_questions: What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize PARP trapping while minimizing toxicity?
Can functional HRD assays (e.g., STRIDE) replace static genomic scar tests for patient selection?

talk_title: Targeting ARID1A/ARID1B synthetic lethality and SMARCA2/SMARCA4 paralog dependencies
MOA_summary: Selective degradation of ARID1B via VHL or cereblon-recruited PROTACs eliminates the compensatory paralog in ARID1A-deficient cancers, leading to loss of BAF complex function and tumor cell death, while sparing normal cells that retain ARID1A. Similarly, inhibition of SMARCA2 in SMARCA4-mutant tumors exploits paralog synthetic lethality, causing chromatin remodeling failure and tumor regression, with enhanced anti‑tumor immunity when combined with PD‑1 blockade.
key_findings: ARID1B degraders achieve nanomolar potency and >10‑fold selectivity over ARID1A in biochemical and cellular assays.
Cereblon‑based ARID1B degraders show superior potency and no off‑target neo‑substrate activity across the proteome.
Ternary complex crystal structures reveal a ligand twist that enhances cooperativity, informing SAR for both VHL and CRBN series.
SMARCA2 inhibitor LY4050784 (909) combined with anti‑PD‑1 yields 6/6 complete responses in a BRG1‑knockout syngeneic mouse model and induces durable immune memory.
Combination of SMARCA2 inhibition with PD‑1 blockade is well tolerated (no weight loss) and produces sustained tumor control.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ARID1B degraders show a clear hook effect at high concentrations, requiring careful dose optimization.
No degradation of ARID1A observed, confirming paralog selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: SMARCA2 inhibition may increase tumor immunogenicity in SMARCA4‑mutant cold tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete tumor regressions and immune memory in mouse model

open_questions: Can ARID1B degraders achieve sufficient oral bioavailability for clinical use?
What biomarkers best predict response to SMARCA2 inhibition beyond SMARCA4 loss?

cited_papers: Ballen et al. 2026 Nature Chemical Biology
